safety_notes: Stay inside the vehicle at all times. Maintain at least 100 m distance. Avoid direct eye contact — it is interpreted as a challenge. Never make sudden movements or loud sounds. Alert your ranger immediately if the animal approaches.

Keep 50–100 m distance. Never block a herd's path or approach a mother with a calf. Mock charge: spread ears and raised trunk — back away slowly. Real charge: pinned ears and lowered head — drive away immediately.
Extreme ambush predator — extremely dangerous if cornered. Maintain at least 50 m. Avoid approaching trees where kills are cached. Never attempt to approach a feeding leopard. Stay in vehicle.
Poor eyesight but acute hearing and smell. Maintain 30–50 m. Move slowly and stay downwind. If it charges, move behind a tree or vehicle. Despite size it can reach 50 km/h.
More aggressive than white rhino. Maintain at least 50 m. Extremely territorial and will charge without warning. Stay in vehicle. Move downwind if on foot — smell is its primary sense.

Considered one of the most dangerous animals in Africa. Unpredictable and known to ambush and circle back on pursuers. Maintain 50 m minimum. Never approach a solitary old bull — they are the most aggressive.

EXTREME DANGER. Causes more human fatalities in Africa than any other large wild animal. Never stand between a hippo and water — it will charge at 30 km/h. Maintain 100 m on land, 200 m on water. Never boat near a pod at night.

EXTREME DANGER near any water. Launches from below waterline with explosive speed. Never approach riverbanks, lake edges, or waterholes in crocodile habitat. Stay at least 5 m from water's edge. Most attacks occur at dawn and dusk.

Lowest big-cat threat level — rarely aggressive toward humans. Maintain 30 m. Do not exit vehicle near a cheetah with cubs. Never disrupt a hunt or a feeding animal — stress causes them to lose their meal.
Generally safe to observe. Zebras bite and kick powerfully when startled or cornered. Maintain 20–30 m. Never approach from behind. Do not separate a zebra from its herd.
Generally non-aggressive. A single kick can be lethal. Maintain 30 m. Give wide berth when driving under or past — vehicles can startle them into kicking.
Generally non-aggressive. Maintain 30 m. A kick from a giraffe can kill a lion — keep distance from legs and do not approach from behind.
Rarely aggressive toward humans. Maintain 30 m. Never disrupt a hunt or approach pups at a den — pack may mob perceived threats. If encountered on foot, stand still and avoid running.

Dangerous in large packs especially at night. Known to approach campsites. Maintain 30 m. Do not feed or approach. Secure all food in camp. If on foot and approached by a pack, make noise and move toward other people.
Shy and rarely aggressive toward humans. Maintain 20 m. Primarily scavenger. Avoid surprising at close range.
Generally not dangerous. May carry rabies — never approach or handle. Maintain 10 m. Do not leave food unattended in camp.
Usually non-aggressive. Will defend itself fiercely if cornered — sharp tusks cause serious wounds. Maintain 10 m. Give way on paths.
Safe to observe in large herds. Can be unpredictable during the Great Migration river crossings — do not stand near riverbanks at crossing points. Maintain 20 m.
Non-aggressive. Safe to observe at any distance. Indicator species — if impalas scatter suddenly, a predator is nearby.
Non-aggressive. Safe to observe. Indicator species — panic flight signals nearby predator. Maintain 10 m to avoid flushing the herd.
Non-aggressive. Safe to observe at any distance.
Non-aggressive. Safe to observe. Often stands on termite mounds as a sentinel — good indicator of predator presence in the area.
Largest antelope — despite size, generally docile. Maintain 20 m. Males have large spiralled horns and can be defensive during rutting season.
Shy and usually flees. Safe to observe at 20 m. Males have impressive spiralled horns — avoid surprising them in dense bush.
Shy and solitary. Males have sharp horns and will defend themselves if cornered. Maintain 10 m. Commonly found near rivers and dense vegetation.
Generally non-aggressive. Males have large curved horns. Maintain 15 m. Often found near rivers and waterholes.
Critically endangered — treat sightings with extreme care. Maintain 50 m to avoid disturbing. Do not approach or attempt to feed.

Large males are dangerous — can cause serious bites. Never feed baboons; they become aggressive and lose fear of humans. Keep car windows closed near baboons. Maintain 10 m. Do not make eye contact with an alpha male.
Rarely dangerous but will bite if fed or cornered. Do not feed. Keep food secured in camp. Maintain 5 m.

Responsible for the most snakebite fatalities in Africa — slow-moving and well-camouflaged. Always watch where you step, especially on paths and near rocks. Never handle. If bitten, immobilise the limb and evacuate immediately.

World's fastest land snake — up to 20 km/h. Highly venomous; untreated bite can be fatal within 20 minutes. Maintain distance. Never corner or approach. If encountered, retreat slowly. Antivenin is essential — ensure medical evacuation plan is in place.
Non-venomous. Bites and claws if handled. Maintain 5 m. Observe from vehicle. Do not provoke.
Non-aggressive toward humans. Safe to observe on foot. Remarkable predator that stamps snakes to death. Keep 20 m to avoid flushing.

Can be aggressive especially during nesting season. A kick from an ostrich can disembowel a human. Maintain 30 m. Do not approach a nesting pair. If threatened it will charge — move behind a vehicle or tree.
Non-aggressive toward humans. Africa's largest eagle. Safe to observe. Nesting pairs will dive-bomb intruders near the nest — maintain 50 m from any active nest.
Non-aggressive. Safe to observe at any distance. One of Africa's most photogenic birds.
Non-aggressive toward humans. Safe to observe. Iconic call is the sound of Africa.
Non-aggressive. Can be bold near campsites — secure food scraps. Large bill is powerful; maintain 5 m.
Bites if handled or approached too closely. Maintain 2 m on designated paths. Do not disturb nesting burrows.

Pound-for-pound the most fearless animal in Africa. Will attack animals many times its size including lions. If encountered on foot, do not approach — move away calmly. Highly defensive and will bite.
Nocturnal and shy. Rarely seen. Non-aggressive. Observe from vehicle if spotted at night.
Shy medium-sized cat. Non-aggressive toward humans. Maintain 20 m to avoid flushing.
Elusive and rarely seen. Non-aggressive toward humans. Maintain 20 m.
Non-aggressive. Resembles a domestic tabby. Safe to observe. Ancestor of the domestic cat.
Critically endangered. Rolls into a ball when threatened. Non-aggressive. If encountered, observe only — do not touch or disturb. Report sightings to conservancy.
Nocturnal and rarely seen. Non-aggressive. Powerful claws for digging — do not corner. Observe from distance if spotted on a night drive.
Thorns are extremely sharp and can puncture tyres and skin. Avoid driving over fallen branches. Do not lean on trunk — Crematogaster ants nest inside thorns and will swarm.
No direct danger. Hollowed trunks can harbour wildlife including bees, pythons, and baboons. Check before approaching.
Highly toxic milky latex — contact with skin causes severe burns; eye contact can cause blindness. Never break or damage. Do not handle branches or sap. Keep children away.
No direct danger. Often grows in malaria-prone areas near water. Take malaria prophylaxis when visiting fever tree forests.

Africa's largest snake — up to 6 m. Non-venomous but kills by constriction. Capable of swallowing prey the size of an impala. Maintain 5 m. Never handle. Especially dangerous near water and in thick vegetation.
